---
title: "01 · Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration"
subtitle: "When the Riemann integral isn't enough — the foundation of modern analysis"
lesson-id: "t7.measure-theory"
tier: 7
prerequisites: ["t5.real-analysis-foundation", "t5.continuous-distributions"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)
   and [T5 L08 — Continuous Distributions](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with $\epsilon$-$\delta$ proofs?
3. Recall Riemann integration limits (Lesson T4 L08).
:::

## Touch

Pick a way to assign sizes to subsets — length on the real line,
count on a discrete set, a spike at one point, total mass 1. The
widget reports what each is good for.

```{ojs}
viewof mtSel = Inputs.select(["Lebesgue measure: μ([a,b]) = b−a", "counting measure: μ(A) = |A|", "Dirac delta: μ({0}) = 1, else 0", "probability measure: μ(X) = 1"], {value: "Lebesgue measure: μ([a,b]) = b−a", label: "Measure:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(mtSel) {
    case "Lebesgue measure: μ([a,b]) = b−a": info = "Standard length on R. Defined via outer measure on intervals, extended via σ-additivity. Foundation of calculus."; break;
    case "counting measure: μ(A) = |A|": info = "On a discrete set: number of elements. Series Σ a_n become Lebesgue integrals over the counting measure."; break;
    case "Dirac delta: μ({0}) = 1, else 0": info = "Concentrated at one point. Integral ∫ f dδ_0 = f(0). Tests for evaluation."; break;
    case "probability measure: μ(X) = 1": info = "Total mass 1. Used in measure-theoretic probability (Kolmogorov): events as measurable sets, probabilities as measure values."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why isn't the Riemann integral "good enough"?
2. The set of rationals in $[0, 1]$ has Lebesgue measure 0 — yet is
   dense. Why?
3. **Lebesgue's monotone / dominated convergence theorems** make
   limits and integrals interchangeable.

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **σ-algebra** $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$ is a collection of subsets
closed under countable unions, intersections, and complements,
containing $\emptyset$ and $X$.

A **measurable space** is $(X, \mathcal{F})$.

A **measure** $\mu: \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty]$ satisfies: $\mu(\emptyset)
= 0$ and **σ-additivity**: $\mu(\bigcup_n A_n) = \sum_n \mu(A_n)$ for
disjoint $A_n$.

Why σ-additivity (not just finite additivity)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**To handle limits properly.** Many natural sets (e.g., the
rationals, decreasing intersections of intervals) only become well-
defined under countable operations.

Example: $\mathbb{Q} \cap [0, 1] = \bigcup_n \{q_n\}$ (countable
union). $\mu(\mathbb{Q}) = \sum \mu(\{q_n\}) = 0$ (each point has
Lebesgue measure 0). Without σ-additivity, this wouldn't follow.
</details>

### Problem 2

The **Lebesgue measure** $\lambda$ on $\mathbb{R}$ is the unique
translation-invariant measure with $\lambda([a, b]) = b - a$ on
intervals.

Compute $\lambda(\mathbb{Q})$ and $\lambda([0, 1] \setminus \mathbb{Q})$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\lambda(\mathbb{Q}) = 0$ (countable union of singletons, each
length 0).

$\lambda([0, 1] \setminus \mathbb{Q}) = \lambda([0, 1]) -
\lambda(\mathbb{Q} \cap [0, 1]) = 1 - 0 = 1$.

**The irrationals in [0, 1] have full measure.** "Almost every"
real is irrational.

This couldn't be expressed in Riemann's framework.
</details>

### Problem 3

The **Lebesgue integral** of a non-negative simple function $\varphi
= \sum c_i \mathbf{1}_{A_i}$:
$$\int \varphi \, d\mu = \sum c_i \mu(A_i).$$

For general $f \ge 0$: $\int f \, d\mu = \sup\{\int \varphi : \varphi
\le f, \varphi \text{ simple}\}$.

Compute $\int_{[0, 1]} \mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}(x) \, d\lambda$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int \mathbf{1}_\mathbb{Q} d\lambda = \lambda(\mathbb{Q} \cap [0, 1])
= 0$.

**The Riemann integral of $\mathbf{1}_\mathbb{Q}$ does not exist**
(upper Riemann sums = 1, lower = 0). But the Lebesgue integral does
— and equals 0.

Lebesgue handles many more functions than Riemann.
</details>

### Problem 4

**Dominated convergence theorem (DCT).** If $f_n \to f$ pointwise and
$|f_n| \le g$ for some integrable $g$, then $\int f_n \to \int f$.

Why is this useful?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Lets you swap limit and integral.** Riemann doesn't always allow
this. DCT is *the* workhorse of advanced analysis.

Sister theorems: **Monotone convergence (MCT)** for increasing
sequences; **Fatou's lemma** for general non-negative.

These three theorems — MCT, DCT, Fatou — are what make Lebesgue
integration so much more flexible than Riemann.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Probability via measure theory** (Kolmogorov, 1933). Replace
"sample space" with measurable space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F})$.
Probability $P$ is a measure with $P(\Omega) = 1$.

Random variables are *measurable functions* $X: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$.
Expected value: $E[X] = \int_\Omega X \, dP$.

Why is this rigour necessary?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**To handle continuous random variables, infinite sequences, and
stochastic processes consistently.** Pre-Kolmogorov probability had
inconsistent foundations (Bertrand's paradox, etc.).

After Kolmogorov: probability is just measure theory with the
constraint $P(\Omega) = 1$. All tools of measure theory apply
seamlessly.

Stochastic calculus (Tier 7+++ for finance), conditional expectation,
martingales — all rest on this foundation.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**σ-algebra** $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$: closed under countable unions,
complements, contains $\emptyset, X$.

**Measure** $\mu: \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty]$: $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$,
σ-additive on disjoint countable unions.

**Lebesgue measure** $\lambda$ on $\mathbb{R}$: translation-invariant,
$\lambda([a, b]) = b - a$.

**Measurable function** $f: X \to \mathbb{R}$: preimages of Borel
sets are in $\mathcal{F}$.

**Lebesgue integral** for non-negative measurable $f$:
$$\int f \, d\mu = \sup\{\int \varphi \, d\mu : \varphi \le f, \varphi \text{ simple}\}.$$

For general $f$: $\int f = \int f^+ - \int f^-$.

**Convergence theorems** (when can we swap $\lim$ and $\int$?):

- **Monotone convergence:** $0 \le f_n \uparrow f \Rightarrow \int f_n
  \to \int f$.
- **Dominated convergence:** $f_n \to f$ pointwise, $|f_n| \le g \in L^1
  \Rightarrow \int f_n \to \int f$.
- **Fatou's lemma:** $\int \liminf f_n \le \liminf \int f_n$ for $f_n
  \ge 0$.

**$L^p$ spaces:** measurable $f$ with $\int |f|^p < \infty$.
Banach spaces. $L^2$ is a Hilbert space.

**Probability axioms (Kolmogorov):** $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$ with
$P(\Omega) = 1$. Random variables = measurable functions.

:::

> *Measure: assigns sizes to sets in a σ-additive way;*
> *Lebesgue integral handles many more functions than Riemann;*
> *DCT, MCT, Fatou: limit-integral swaps;*
> *Kolmogorov: probability = measure theory with P(Ω) = 1.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $\int_0^\infty x e^{-x} dx$ using Lebesgue (or Riemann —
> both agree here).

Integration by parts: $\int x e^{-x} dx = -x e^{-x} - e^{-x} + C$.
Evaluated $0$ to $\infty$: $0 - (0 - 1) = 1$.

(For "nice" functions, Riemann and Lebesgue agree. Lebesgue's
advantage is for pathological cases and for the convergence theorems.)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Lebesgue measure of $\{1, 2, 3\}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0$ (finite, hence countable, set).
</details>

**W2.** Is the Riemann integral of $\mathbf{1}_\mathbb{Q}$ defined?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No.** Lebesgue integral is.
</details>

**W3.** Probability that $X \in \mathbb{Q}$ for $X \sim N(0, 1)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0$ (since $\lambda(\mathbb{Q}) = 0$ and the normal is absolutely
continuous w.r.t. Lebesgue).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show $\int_0^1 \mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}(x) dx = 0$
(Lebesgue).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By Discover P3.
</details>

**P2.** Apply DCT to compute $\lim_{n \to \infty} \int_0^1 (1 -
x/n)^n dx$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(1 - x/n)^n \to e^{-x}$ pointwise. Bounded by 1 on $[0, 1]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int_0^1 e^{-x} dx = 1 - e^{-1} \approx 0.632$.

DCT lets us swap: $\lim_n \int = \int \lim_n$.
</details>

**P3.** What's an $L^2$ function?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Measurable $f$ with $\int |f|^2 < \infty$. Forms a Hilbert space —
foundation of Fourier analysis, quantum mechanics.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Why is the Lebesgue integral the "right" generalisation?**
What does it gain over Riemann?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Properties Riemann lacks:**

1. *Limit-integral swaps* (DCT, MCT, Fatou). Riemann needs uniform
   convergence, often too strong.
2. *Completeness:* $L^p$ spaces are Banach. Riemann-integrable
   functions form *incomplete* spaces under $L^p$ norms.
3. *Wide function class:* characteristic functions of arbitrary
   measurable sets, almost-everywhere defined functions.
4. *Probability foundation:* Kolmogorov axiomatisation only works
   with measures.

Modern probability, functional analysis, harmonic analysis — all
require Lebesgue.
</details>

**S2.** **Connection to ML.** Many ML quantities are integrals over
high-dimensional spaces. Why is measure theory the right framework?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Universal language for "probabilities" and "expectations".**
Whether your random variable is discrete, continuous, mixed, or
defined on weird spaces (functions, neural network parameter
spaces) — measure theory handles all of them uniformly.

**Modern ML uses:**
- Probability measures on weight spaces (Bayesian deep learning).
- Wasserstein distance (Tier 7+) for comparing distributions
  (GANs, optimal transport).
- Pushforward measures (how distributions transform under maps).

Without measure theory, deep theoretical work in ML wouldn't be
possible.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Lebesgue replaces Riemann."** No — Lebesgue *generalises*. For
   nice functions (continuous, piecewise smooth), they agree.
2. **"Almost every" is colloquial.** It means "except on a set of
   measure zero" — a precise mathematical concept.
3. **"All sets are measurable."** Not in $\mathbb{R}$ under
   Lebesgue + axiom of choice (Vitali set). Tier 7+++ explores
   non-measurable sets.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state σ-additivity?
2. Without scrolling back, what does DCT say?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: limit and integral can be swapped under domination.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[02 · Topology](../02-topology/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (Lebesgue's advantages).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)**
- **[T5 L08 — Continuous Distributions](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- Functional analysis on $L^p$ spaces.
- Stochastic processes, martingales.
- Geometric measure theory.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** *The* language of modern analysis,
probability, and ergodic theory.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Generative models (S2), Bayesian
inference, optimal transport (Wasserstein GANs), measure-valued
neural networks.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Quantitative finance.** Stochastic calculus, martingale theory,
risk-neutral pricing — all built on measure-theoretic probability.
:::
